Add a city like Istanbul, where you can recover between two continents, with the Bosphorus on one side and centuries of history on the other, and it becomes clear why so many people turn a medical appointment into a genuine trip.But choosing a clinic from abroad is harder than choosing a hotel. You are trusting a team you have never met with a result you will carry for the rest of your life. Article Title: Auto industry analysts are worried about Tesla and other car makers using the technology that Google, Apple, Samsung and other smartphone companies have been using since over ten years to update their
Article Text:
OTA updates raise cybersecurity concerns: Analyst



Norwegian bus test highlights potential risks



Auto makers are increasingly using over-the-air (OTA) software updates, a technology that smartphone companies like Google, Apple and Samsung have relied on for more than a decade to deliver new features and security fixes. While the technology has made updating vehicles faster and cheaper, cybersecurity experts are warning that it also creates new risks. According to a CNBC report, analysts say wireless updates could leave connected vehicles vulnerable to cyberattacks, data breaches and even attempts by foreign actors to interfere with vehicle systems.Over-the-air (OTA) technology allows manufacturers to wirelessly send software updates, bug fixes and new features to internet-connected devices without requiring a visit to a service center.Tesla began rolling out OTA updates for its Model S vehicles in 2012, helping popularize the technology across the automotive industry. Today, many car makers use OTA updates to improve vehicle software and fix issues remotely."The technology is increasingly welcomed as it is a quick and cost-effective way to manage systems on vehicles, over traditional methods which may have required a recall or update at routine maintenance," Siraj Ahmed Shaikh, professor in systems security at Swansea University in the UK, told CNBC.However, analysts say the growing use of OTA updates also introduces new cybersecurity risks.Its use represents "a unique national security concern," Gabriel Lim, senior analyst at the S. Rajaratnam School of International Studies in Singapore, told CNBC."Aside from data privacy concerns, the potential of a foreign actor sabotaging the controls of a moving vehicle is a possibility that countries like Norway, Denmark, and Britain have expressed concerns about," Lim added.The concerns gained attention after Norwegian public transport company Ruter tested two buses last year and found potential security risks linked to OTA technology."There is access to the control system for battery and power supply via mobile network through a Romanian SIM card. In theory, therefore, this bus can be stopped or rendered inoperable by the manufacturer," the company said.The findings prompted authorities in the UK and Denmark to launch their own investigations.
Article Summary: Auto makers are increasingly using over-the-air (OTA) software updates. The technology allows manufacturers to send software updates, bug fixes and new features to internet-connected devices without requiring a visit to a service center. But cybersecurity experts are warning that it also creates new risks.

Article Title: Pentagon hit by AI's 'biggest problem' after the whole Army reportedly burned through the whole year of tokens for just one service
Article Text:
The Pentagon

Army token pool runs empty



Reliability concerns amid rollouts



Just weeks after highlighting that nearly half of its 3.5 million military and civilian personnel have been using artificial intelligence (AI) on the job, the Department of War (formerly Department of Defense) has hit a massive computational wall. The US Army has reportedly burned through its entire annual budget of AI processing “tokens” in a matter of weeks, forcing military leaders to re-impose strict usage caps across departments, a report has said.According to Wired, the shortage highlights growing concern over corporate and governmental ‘overconsumption’ of generative AI tools even as other organisations struggle to manage skyrocketing cloud computing costs.In May 2026, the Army Chief Information Officer (CIO) announced that personnel would be granted virtually unlimited access to generative AI. However, internal emails sent to members of the Army’s Combat Capabilities Development Command (DEVCOM) revealed a sharp reversal by mid-June.“Although the Army CIO announced in May 2026 that they were offering unlimited tokens, by mid-June the Army CIO pool was exhausted of tokens and had to re-establish limits.Apparently the whole Army burned through the whole year of tokens for just one service,” an Army insider told WIRED.The service in question is Ask Sage: a secure, accredited platform that allows military personnel to run top commercial AI models, including OpenAI’s ChatGPT, Alphabet’s Gemini and Meta’s Llama. The AI models are being used for tasks like rewriting job descriptions and processing procurement acquisitions.Previously, to encourage adoption, the Army initially granted workers at least 200,000 tokens per month and automatically bumped up allowances for heavy users. They even sent reminder emails to inactive employees urging them to use their allotments. That aggressive push led to rapid exhaustion of the Army's annual 100-million-token “enterprise pack.”Despite token depletion, the Pentagon continues to integrate AI into sensitive workflows, the report said, indicating that the DOW recently cut staff at the Civilian Protection Center of Excellence, opting instead to develop automated AI software to evaluate civilian casualty risks in active combat zones.However, rank-and-file personnel express skepticism about whether the current generation of AI tools is ready for prime-time military deployment.
Article Summary: The US Army has reportedly burned through its entire annual budget of AI processing ‘tokens’ in a matter of weeks. The shortage highlights growing concern over corporate and governmental ‘overconsumption’ of generative AI tools.

Article Title: Adobe adds Google's Nano Banana-powered AI editor to Indigo camera app
Article Text:
What the AI Playground actually does inside Indigo

Free, but only for a few percent, and your photo goes to the cloud

Adobe has added a generative AI editor right inside its experimental Project Indigo camera app, letting mobile photographers stylise, clean up, and critique their shots seconds after pressing the shutter. The new feature, called AI Playground, arrives a year after the Nextcam team first launched Indigo with manual controls, an SLR-like look, and tricks like removing reflections when shooting through windows.The pitch is simple: editing in the moment, while you're still standing in front of the scene. Adobe argues that text prompts alone are a clumsy way to steer image editors—even a precise prompt is ambiguous, and you often need several attempts to land a good result. So instead of making you type everything, Indigo offers ready-made buttons for the most common jobs, backed by prompts the team has engineered and tested.The Playground splits into four sections—Object Editing, Styles, Photo Guidance, and Custom Edit. You reach it by opening a captured photo and tapping the sparkle button at the bottom. The catch: it only works on photos shot in Indigo, so the button greys out for anything else.Object Editing handles distractor removal and fake shallow depth of field, with toggles to strip out specific categories of clutter—and you can add your own by typing or dictating. Styles re-renders your snapshot using various artistic techniques, and can even run in reverse, turning a painting or bronze sculpture you're photographing into a "photo." Photo Guidance is the clever bit for a camera app: it critiques your shot and suggests both reshoots and edits, tuned to know your phone can't optically zoom or change aperture. Custom Edit opens the door to any prompt you want.Access is free with no sign-on, but Adobe is only handing it to a few percent of users for a few weeks as a learning experiment. Take part and you agree to share your button presses—though Adobe says it won't view or upload your prompts or images, and the analytics stay anonymous. A paid version may follow if it catches on.Under the hood, the app starts with Google's Nano Banana model, with Adobe's own Firefly possibly stepping in for cleaner distractor removal. Since Nano Banana is cloud-based, you need internet, and results come back around 2K pixels. Because these models output SDR, Adobe built a custom ML model to boost images back to HDR using your original capture as a guide.Marc Levoy, who led development alongside Mohammad Haque, Boris Ajdin, and Ruiming Cao, frames it honestly—these models are hard to control, can shift facial identity, and stay slow and cloud-bound. But the appeal, he says, is having all this a thumb-reach from the shutter.
Article Summary: Adobe has added a generative AI editor right inside its experimental Project Indigo camera app. The feature, called AI Playground, lets mobile photographers stylise, clean up, and critique their shots seconds after pressing the shutter. Access is free with no sign-on, but Adobe is only handing it to a few percent of users for a few weeks as a learning experiment.

Article Title: Sam Altman’s OpenAI paused internal access to AI model after unexpected safety issues
Article Text:
OpenAI model found ways to bypass restrictions

Sam Altman-led OpenAI has revealed that it paused internal access to one of its experimental AI models after it showed unexpected behaviour during testing. In an official blog post, the ChatGPT-maker said the model, designed to work on long-running tasks without human intervention, carried out actions that were not caught by its existing safety tests. “During limited internal use of a model trained for long-running tasks, we observed novel failures not captured in our existing pre-deployment evaluations and paused access,” the ChatGPT-maker said.The company said it stopped internal deployment, built new safeguards and monitoring tools, and later restored limited access after improving the model. “We then used insights from these failures to build new evaluations, improve long-horizon alignment, add trajectory-level monitoring, and give users greater visibility and control before restoring limited access”.OpenAI revealed that it announced to develop a model that disproved the Erdős unit distance conjecture. This model was designed to work autonomously for very long periods of time. But during that time, the company “observed unwanted behavior that our existing deployment evaluations had not captured”. OpenAI later identified these problems and paused access, before restoring access under continued monitoring."The new model can continue working toward an objective through repeated attempts over a long period of time," OpenAI said in a blog post on safety. "That same persistence can lead it to find and exploit weaknesses in its environment. Previous models, when they hit sandboxing or environmental constraints, would simply stop and return to the user. This model often kept trying, including by looking for ways to act outside its sandbox. "In the blog post, the AI company said that it plans to increase the trajectories of model testing, limit access to those using the models, pause when problems emerge to build stronger safeguards, and restore limited access after testing the changes."As models take on longer and more complex tasks, failures that evaluations miss may carry greater consequences," OpenAI said. "We will keep working to narrow the gap between evaluation and deployment: testing models over longer trajectories, improving alignment, building monitoring that can intervene, and giving users clearer visibility and control. These challenges will not be unique to OpenAI, and we hope sharing what we learned helps the broader field prepare for them. "
Article Summary: OpenAI paused internal access to one of its experimental AI models after it showed unexpected behaviour during testing. The model was designed to work on long-running tasks without human intervention. The company said it stopped internal deployment, built new safeguards and monitoring tools, and later restored limited access after improving the model.

Article Title: Google employees' Union president gives what is called 'never-before' petition to CEO Sundar Pichai; says: Make no mistake that this is a company that is...
Article Text:
Google layoffs petition delivered to Sundar Pichai's office behind closed doors



Alphabet Workers Union shifts from Google ethics protests to AI-era job security

Google voluntary exit packages: The one union win keeping the campaign alive

Nearly 100 Google employees gathered on a grassy stretch of the company's Mountain View campus on Thursday, sandwiched between the Googleplex and the visitor centre, holding signs that read "Googlers for Job Security. " Many wore matching black shirts. Others unfurled a long white banner carrying the names of more than 4,500 employees who had signed a petition demanding stronger protection against layoffs—a document the Alphabet Workers Union describes as the largest single piece of employee feedback Google has ever received on job security.The petition, addressed to CEO Sundar Pichai and three senior executives, asks for guaranteed severance, voluntary exit packages before any forced cuts, an end to performance quotas, and the option to take severance as extended paid leave. Union president Parul Koul didn't mince words about why the fight matters at a company sitting on a $4 trillion valuation. "Make no mistake: this is a company that is enjoying massive, unprecedented success," she told the crowd. "These layoffs and cuts are not difficult decisions, but simply profit being put over the people that make this company run."The delivery began early. Around 9 a.m., roughly 20 workers walked the petition to the offices of Google Cloud CEO Thomas Kurian and senior vice presidents Rick Osterloh and Nick Fox, before heading to Pichai's office.None of the executives were there. So the group slipped copies under their doors and left the rest with a staff member who promised to pass it along. Koul said employees tried to hand Pichai a version last year, got no real response, and came back with more than double the signatures.For a workforce famous for pushing back on ethics—Project Maven in 2018, the 20,000-strong walkout over sexual-misconduct handling, the Project Nimbus sit-ins—the shift toward bread-and-butter job security is telling. Google cut 12,000 jobs in 2023 and has run several smaller rounds since, leaving employees bracing for the next notice. Software engineer Nobel Barakat put it plainly at the rally: he sees people grateful to still have work, quietly grinding longer days in the hope of dodging a sudden poor rating.The AI build-out looms over all of it. Alphabet has pledged massive capital spending on data centres, and management has begun folding "AI fluency" into performance metrics—criteria that engineer Dan Freedman says feel underspecified and vaguely threatening. After AI landed in his job requirements, he told reporters he worries he isn't using it enough and could be replaced. "I have to wonder if I'm next," he said.The union isn't starting from zero. Since the campaign began, Google has offered voluntary exit packages to more than 70,000 employees—a program Pichai has publicly credited to listening to staff, saying it gives people agency. Koul notes that figure counts those eligible, not those who accepted. Still, no other large tech company has adopted it, and the union treats it as proof that organising works.As TV cameras rolled, workers raised their signs and chanted: "Google, Google, can't you see? We deserve security." Google did not immediately respond to requests for comment. AWU, currently a minority union of around 1,400 members, says the pressure won't let up.
Article Summary: More than 4,500 Google employees signed a petition demanding stronger job security. The petition was delivered to CEO Sundar Pichai's office behind closed doors on Thursday. It asks for guaranteed severance, voluntary exit packages before any forced cuts, an end to performance quotas.
